Other Formats

  • RevealJS

Exercise 2: GitHub Classroom - Push, Pull, and Conflicts

For this exercise, we’ve prepared a GitHub Classroom repository. GitHub Classroom creates a personal copy of a template repository for each student - perfect for practicing collaboration scenarios.

Part A: Clone and First Push

1. Accept the invitation and clone

Visit the classroom invitation link: https://classroom.github.com/a/YOUR_LINK_HERE

After accepting, clone your personal repository:

  • Windows
  • Mac
cd ~/OneDrive/Documents/Introduction_to_programming/exercises/week_03
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_ORG/git-practice-YOUR_USERNAME.git
cd git-practice-YOUR_USERNAME
cd ~/Documents/Introduction_to_programming/exercises/week_03
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_ORG/git-practice-YOUR_USERNAME.git
cd git-practice-YOUR_USERNAME

2. Examine the initial file

Open some_arithmetic.py in your editor. You’ll see:

"""
Some Arithmetic
===============
Practice with expressions
"""

# TODO: Define variable 'a' using an arithmetic expression
a = None

# Pre-defined variables
b = 20 + 22
c = sum([40, 2])

# Print results
print("Result for a:", a)
print("Result for b:", b)
print("Result for c:", c)

3. Define variable a and push

Replace a = None with an arithmetic expression (any expression you like, e.g., a = 6 * 7).

git status
git add some_arithmetic.py
git commit -m "Define variable a"
git push
Note

What happens next: After you push, a GitHub Action will automatically run and add a test block to your file. This simulates a teammate pushing changes while you’re working.

Part B: Pulling Changes

4. Pull the automated changes

Wait about 30 seconds, then pull:

git pull

You should see output like:

remote: Enumerating objects: 5, done.
...
Updating abc1234..def5678
Fast-forward
 some_arithmetic.py | 6 ++++++
 1 file changed, 6 insertions(+)

5. Examine the new test block

Open some_arithmetic.py again. A test block has been added:

# Test block
if a == b == c:
    print("✓ All values are equal to 42!")
else:
    print("✗ Not all values are 42 yet")

6. Make a equal 42 and push again

Looking at the test, all three variables should equal 42. Since b = 42 and c = 42 already, change your expression for a to also equal 42 (e.g., a = 6 * 7).

git add some_arithmetic.py
git commit -m "Fix variable a to equal 42"
git push
Note

What happens next: The automation will now modify variable b on the remote. This sets up a merge conflict scenario.

Part C: Creating and Resolving a Merge Conflict

This is the most important part - learning to handle conflicts when you and a collaborator modify the same code.

7. Modify variable b locally (don’t push yet!)

Before pulling, modify b in your local file to a different expression that also equals 42:

b = 30 + 12  # Or any other expression that equals 42

Commit this change locally only:

git add some_arithmetic.py
git commit -m "Change variable b to a different expression"
Warning

Do NOT push yet! We want to create a conflict scenario.

8. Try to pull - encounter the conflict

Now try to pull the remote changes:

git pull

You’ll see a merge conflict:

Auto-merging some_arithmetic.py
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in some_arithmetic.py
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.

9. Examine the conflict

Open some_arithmetic.py. You’ll see conflict markers:

<<<<<<< HEAD
b = 30 + 12  # Your local change
=======
b = 21 * 2  # Modified by automation
>>>>>>> abc1234...

Understanding conflict markers:

  • <<<<<<< HEAD: Your local changes start here
  • =======: Separator between local and remote changes
  • >>>>>>> abc1234: Remote changes end here (commit hash)

10. Resolve the conflict

You must choose which version to keep, or create a new one. For this exercise, choose your local version (b = 30 + 12).

  • Using VS Code
  • Manual editing
  1. Click “Accept Current Change” (keeps your version)
  2. Or manually delete the conflict markers and keep only your line
  3. Save the file

Delete the conflict markers and remote line:

# Remove everything and keep only:
b = 30 + 12

11. Complete the merge

After resolving the conflict:

git add some_arithmetic.py
git commit -m "Resolve merge conflict in variable b"
git push

Check your commit history:

git log --oneline --graph

You should see a merge commit in your history!

Reflection Questions

Think about it
  1. Why did the conflict occur?
    • Both you and the automation modified the same line (b = ...)
    • Git couldn’t automatically decide which version to keep
  2. When do conflicts NOT occur?
    • When changes are in different parts of the file
    • When changes are in different files entirely
  3. Real-world scenario: When might this happen with actual teammates?
    • Two people fixing the same bug differently
    • Refactoring the same function simultaneously
    • Working on a feature without pulling recent changes
  4. How to avoid conflicts?
    • Pull frequently before starting work
    • Communicate with teammates about who’s working on what
    • Use feature branches (covered in next week’s lecture)

Summary

You’ve now experienced the complete Git collaboration workflow:

  1. ✓ Clone a repository
  2. ✓ Push your changes
  3. ✓ Pull collaborator changes
  4. ✓ Resolve conflicts when changes overlap
  5. ✓ Merge and continue working

These skills are essential for the group project and real-world software development!
